# Low Frequency Radio Astronomy

Steven Tremblay (NRAO)

# Low Frequency: What do we mean by ‘Low’?

Credit: SKAO

# Low Frequency: What do we mean by ‘Low’?

Credit: Adapted from NASA

Radio Spectrum (ITU):

Very Low Frequency (3 kHz – 30 kHz )

Low Frequency (30 kHz – 300 kHz)

Medium Frequency (300 kHz – 3 MHz)

High Frequency (3 MHz – 30 MHz)

Very High Frequency (30 MHz – 300 MHz)

Ultra High Frequency (300 MHz – 3 GHz)

Super High Frequency (3 GHz – 30 GHz)

Extremely High Frequency (30 GHz – 300 GHz)

(ULF)

(ELF)

(SLF)
# Low Frequency: The Good, the Bad, and the Ugly

# Low Frequency: The Good , the Bad, and the Ugly

The Good:

Large Field of View ( FoV )

Fantastic for Survey Science!

Sensitive to large-scale structure

Resolution: where 30cm – 30m

Simpler Systems

Not all systems need mechanical pointing

Often can digitize from DC up to frequency (No Local Oscillator)

Reduced maintenance (for reasons above)

Fantastic for hands on learning

Less expensive*

Can be easier to build large collecting area

# Low Frequency: The Good, the Bad , and the Ugly

The Bad:

Can have highly direction dependent gains

MWA example on right

Can be computationally expensive

Therefore possibly actually expensive (or evens out)

Wide FoV means more affected by source confusion

Challenging to calibrate

Can be RFI prevalent environment

Satellites

Powerlines

Lightning

Requires massive baselines for high resolution: The inherent trade-off of the relationship.

120.96 MHz

156.56 MHz

210.56 MHz

Meyers et al. 2017

# Low Frequency: The Good, the Bad, and the Ugly

The Ugly:

Ionosphere

“After you find a bigger task to dread, the little chores will be a breeze. Here’s another reason to have a devil on hand. It does make all the little demons more . . . bearable.” ― Chuck Palahniuk, Haunted

# Region of atmosphere between ~80 and ~600 km where extreme ultraviolet (EUV) and x-ray solar radiation ionizes the atoms and molecules thus creating a layer of electrons

Ionosphere

Brown & Lombry 2004

# Region of atmosphere between ~80 and ~600 km where extreme ultraviolet (EUV) and x-ray solar radiation ionizes the atoms and molecules thus creating a layer of electrons

Time variable on multiple time-scales

(Years) 11 year Solar Activity Cycle

(Hours) Day/Night

(Minutes) Space Weather and TIDs

Ionosphere

Brown & Lombry 2004

# Ionosphere is a charged plasma that your radio waves propagate through

What does this mean in practice? (Roughly)

Incoming waves are reflected (below 10MHz)

Incoming waves are refracted (10MHz – 10GHz)

Source Position Shifts / Image Distortion

Scintillates Flux Densities

Also plays havoc with the polarimetry (via Faraday Rotation)

Incoming waves are not noticeably impacted (above 10GHz)

Ionosphere: What does it do?

# Ionosphere: What can we do?

Spatial Structure

Simple slab: Uniform phase offset for narrow fields of view

Wedge: Phase gradient to model source position shifts

Zernike polynomials: Maps higher-order ripples and image distortion

Temporal Evolution

Ionosphere is highly dynamic

Spatial models must continuously track moving turbulent cells

Calibration Strategy

Can use position offsets and image distortions as your input

Corrections applied to:

Visibilities

Direction dependent gains to ( u,v ) plane

Directly to the (l, m) image grid

# Ionosphere

Vector source offsets (154 MHz)

Chege et al. 2021

# Ionosphere

Credit: Chris Jordan

# Low frequency Calibration

Direction dependent gains must be accounted for:

‘Easy’ to model based on simulations/empirical data

Ionosphere must be tamed

FoV actually helps

Confusion needs to be dealt with:

Peeling and its successors

All of these add to increased computational burden

Credit: C. Tremblay

Pulsars (Surveys, MSPs, Single-pulse)

Shocks / Turbulence / Jets (Active Galactic Nuclei, Supernova Remnants)

Transients (Fast Radio Bursts, Tidal Disruption Events)

Solar / Heliospheric

Survey Science

Ionospheric*

Cosmic Ray

Radio Recombination Lines / Spectral Lines

Interstellar / Interplanetary Medium

Serendipity

Etc.

# Imaging:

Centaurus A imaged with the MWA 32 MHz bandwidth centered @154 MHz

9 deg extent

‘Science’ images from 112 seconds of data in a ‘drift scan’ mode

McKinley & Matherne 2021

McKinley et al. 2017

PhD Thesis

Galactic Center imaged with the MWA

Part of spectral line search

Nominal detections of NO

C. Tremblay et al. 2017

# Low frequency science

Pulsars: Spectral Index

Turnover currently poorly understood

Important clues to understanding pulsar emission mechanism

Other consequences:

Great frequency range to survey pulsars

Allows more single-pulse studies of individual pulsars

# Low frequency science

J0034-0702

Credit: Sammy McSweeney

Single-pulse analysis of a ‘sub-pulse drifter’ with MWA

Not all pulsars exhibit this behavior

Ones that do often have multiple ‘modes’

These can also exhibit ‘nulling’, consecutive spin rotations where no emission is detected

McSweeney et al. 2017 and subsequent papers

# Low frequency science

Pulsars: Scattering

Low freq. best way to study scattering

Gives critical Interstellar Medium insight

Also an important limitation to pulsar observations along with dispersion

# Low frequency science

Solar/ Heliospheric : Interplanetary Scintillation (IPS)

Imaged 900deg 2

79 & 158 MHz

350 scintillating sources

4x real-time

Morgan et al. 2008
# Low Frequency: Instruments

# Low Frequency Legacy

Credit: Bell Telephone Laboratories

The Beginning: Proof of Concept

K.G. Jansky (1932): The "Merry-Go-Round" bare dipole array (20.5 MHz). Discovered the steady hiss of the Galactic Center.

Grote Reber (1937): Transitioned to a dish architecture (160 MHz, 900 MHz, 3.3 GHz).

The Middle: Scaling the Arrays

Bernard Mills (1954): The 'Mills Cross' (85.5 MHz). Utilized massive 450m static arms to achieve resolution.

Bill Erickson (1960s-1970s): Clark Lake Radio Observatory (20–125 MHz). Championed massive static arrays over massive moving dishes.
# Low Frequency Instruments: Dishes

4 band (56-88 MHz)

P band (240-470 MHz)

VLA Low-band Ionosphere and Transient Experiment (VLITE) operating 24/7 at P band on 18 VLA antennas

# Low frequency instruments: LWA

10-88 MHz

256 dual-pol angled dipoles

Stations designed to be used independently or combined

Sky-noise dominated

# Low frequency Instruments: LWA

LWA1 Construction 2009

LWA-CS Surveying 2024

# Low frequency instruments: LWA

LWA Swarm: mini-stations

64 antennas distributed within 80m diameter

# Low frequency instruments: MWA (Recently Upgraded)

Murchison, Australia

70-300 MHz

Consists of ‘Tiles’

4x4 grid of cross-dipoles

256 Tiles

‘ Reconfigurable’

Compact: ~800 m

Extended: ~5 km

Both of these previous ‘configurations’ can be simultaneously correlated now with upgraded MWAX correlator

# Low frequency instruments: LOFAR (Recently Upgraded)

Europe

Netherlands centered

10-80 MHz (LBA)

120-240 MHz (HBA)

Can now correlate both simultaneously!

52 Stations (+2 more soon)

Core & Remote stations consist of 48 HBA's and 96 LBA’s

International stations consist of 96 HBA’s and 96 LBA’s

# Low frequency instruments: LOFAR

LOFAR is a dedicated low frequency very long baseline interferometer

See Justin Linford’s VLBI talk!
# Low Frequency Instruments: The Future…

# Low frequency instruments: LuSEE -Night

0.1-50 MHz

4 monopole antennas in cross formation

Far side of the Moon

Late 2026

NASA & DoE collaboration

Study ‘Dark Ages’

ITU’s Shielded Zone of the Moon regulations (0-30 MHz, 300-2000 MHz)

# Low frequency instruments: CHORD

Canadian Hydrogen Observatory and Radio-transient Detector

Frequency: 300 to 1500 MHz

Architecture: 512 ultra-wideband 6m dishes tightly packed in a core, plus continental outriggers.

Science: Next-generation mapping of cosmic structure and ultra-precise FRB localization (an order of magnitude leap over CHIME).

Status: Currently under construction.

# Low frequency instruments; SKA-Low

50 MHz - 350 MHz

Western Australia

131,072 log-periodic antennas spread between 512 stations

Collecting area: 419,000m 2

Extent: 74km

Phase

Milestone / Objective

Target Timeline

AA0.5

First 4 stations (1,024 antennas) working together.

Completed (2024-2025)

AA1

18 stations acting as a fully functional interferometer.

Ongoing (2026)

AA2 (First Light)

64 stations online. This serves as the major "Science Verification" milestone where the community can propose early test science.

Expected ~2027

Full Operations

All 512 stations online and fully integrated.

Expected ~2028–2029

# Low Frequency Radio Astronomy

Really easy…but also kinda hard

Fantastic regime to learn Radio Astronomy in a ‘hands on’ environment

Ionosphere can dominate errors

MHz regime opens up new areas of study for many science topics

This is an amazing age for <1GHz Radio Astronomy with a plethora of instruments across the globe

Use the survey data that’s being gifted to you

Have fun with it!

SKA-Low AA0.5 First Image ( March 2025): Using the first 4 stations (1,024 antennas). The 7-hour integration at 163 MHz captures a 25-square-degree field centered on PKS 0521-36, mapping ~85 galaxies. (Moon added for scale)

# Low Frequency Radio Astronomy

Thank You
